For testing, I used a sample SM58 text export that contains DOC_TYPE as PDF and a BINARYARCHIVOBJECT table with binary data.

The PDF content begins with 25504446, which is the hexadecimal representation of %PDF.

The extracted sample PDF opens correctly and contains the simple test message:

"This is a test PDF document."

That was a nice moment.

Not because the sample PDF is exciting.

It is not.

It is probably the most boring PDF in the world.

But boring test PDFs are perfect when you are testing extractors. They do not complain, they do not have missing customizing, and they do not open SAP incidents.

Limitations

This tool does not read SM58 directly from SAP.

You still need to save the LUW display as a local text file first.

It also does not fix the original ArchiveLink customizing issue. If the real root cause is missing content repository setup, missing document type assignment, or another ArchiveLink configuration problem, this extractor will not solve that.

It only helps with one very specific task:

Take the binary data already visible in the SM58 LUW display and reconstruct it into a local file.

That is all.

But sometimes that is exactly what you need.

GitHub repository:

https://github.com/kmodric/LUW_extractor/blob/main/luw_extractor_web.html 

Conclusion

So now the LUW Extractor story has three chapters:

- Part I: C# version
- Part II: ABAP version
- Part III: HTML/JavaScript browser version

Same problem.

Same SAP kitchen.

Different cooking equipment.

The browser version is probably the easiest one to share and use, because it is just one HTML file. It does not replace proper SAP troubleshooting, but it gives you a fast and practical way to inspect what is hidden inside an SM58 LUW text export.

And honestly, that is the kind of small tool I like the most.

Not a huge framework.

Not a six-month project.

Just a tiny helper that saves time when you really need it.

Feel free to test it, break it, improve it, or share a better way to achieve the same result.

Thanks everyone for reading.  😃

entry.title: Rethinking Transformation: Why Procurement Is Leading the Next ERP Evolution 🚀
entry.content:
Rethinking Transformation: Why Procurement Is Leading the Next ERP Evolution 🚀

Digital transformation has become a familiar term, but for many organizations it still feels abstract, risky, or overwhelming. In a recent episode of The Future of Procurement, Curt Reppart, Associate Partner at IBM, offered a grounded and experience-driven perspective on how companies can modernize procurement and ERP landscapes without losing momentum—or value—along the way. 💡

Drawing on more than a decade in SAP consulting and procurement transformation, Reppart framed a compelling message: successful transformation is less about technology choices and more about behavior, data, and intentional change.

Beyond Greenfield and Brownfield: The Rise of Selective Transformation 🔄

One of the most valuable insights from the discussion was the clear explanation of ERP transformation paths. Traditionally, organizations have faced a binary choice: brownfield (lift-and-shift the existing system as-is) or greenfield (start fresh with standard processes). While each has merits, both extremes come with trade-offs—either carrying forward technical debt or absorbing massive disruption. ⚖️

Reppart highlighted the growing adoption of selective transformation, often referred to as “bluefield.” This approach blends technical conversion with deliberate business decisions about what data, processes, and configurations to keep, redesign, or retire. The result is a more pragmatic migration to S/4HANA that balances speed, cost, and long-term flexibility. 🧭

Procurement as the Ideal Transformation Entry Point 🛒

Why does procurement consistently emerge as a strong candidate for transformation? According to Reppart, procurement processes should be inherently standardized across industries, yet they are often among the most over-customized areas in legacy ERP environments. 🧩

Years of workarounds, exception handling, and disconnected supplier communication have created unnecessary complexity. Modern procurement platforms—particularly those leveraging supplier networks—offer an opportunity to simplify internal workflows while dramatically improving external collaboration. 🌐

Reppart emphasized that true transformation extends beyond the four walls of the enterprise. Supplier enablement, compliance, and real-time collaboration are no longer “nice-to-haves”; they are foundational to resilience and scalability.

Transformation Is About People, Not Just Platforms 👥

A recurring theme throughout the conversation was the importance of changing behaviors, not just systems. New technology alone will not fix broken processes. In fact, without behavioral change, organizations often recreate old problems on new platforms. 🔁

Reppart stressed the need to engage stakeholders at multiple levels—from the C-suite defining outcomes, to mid-level leaders owning transformation decisions, to frontline users who understand how work truly gets done. Listening to those closest to the process often reveals hidden dependencies and manual steps that formal documentation overlooks. 🎧

This grassroots insight is critical in determining which processes are stable, which are held together by “band-aids,” and where transformation will deliver the greatest value.

Data as the Foundation for AI-Driven Procurement 🤖

Looking ahead, Reppart offered a pragmatic take on artificial intelligence in procurement. While AI is now part of every executive conversation, its success depends entirely on data quality and information architecture(IA). “You can’t have AI without IA,” he noted. 📊

As AI agents and automation capabilities mature, procurement professionals will rely less on deep system navigation skills and more on the ability to ask the right questions—effectively becoming prompt-driven operators. Over time, interfaces may matter less than outcomes, with users interacting through a single conversational layer that orchestrates multiple intelligent agents behind the scenes. 🧠

The Road Ahead: Progress Over Perfection 🛤️

Reppart’s closing perspective was clear: transformation is not about being first or perfect, but about being intentional. Organizations that invest early in clean core principles, strong data models, and scalable processes will be better positioned to adopt future innovations—whether AI-driven or otherwise. ✅

Procurement, with its direct impact on cost, risk, and supplier relationships, is uniquely positioned to lead this journey. And as Reppart underscored, modernization isn’t about starting over—it’s about knowing what to take with you. 🔍

Apple: Listen on Apple!

Spotify: Listen on Spotify!
